requirements.statement: Domain-name input is normalized before validation — uppercase plus strip ALL whitespace; spaces are never converted to dashes.
requirements.why: A dash is HEAD_RE's key/seq separator; a dashed key scaffolds a spec that silently parses to zero requirements.

requirements.statement: When normalization changed the input, spec domain new prints exactly one normalized "<raw>" → <KEY> line; canonical input prints none.
requirements.why: The user must see the transformation that was applied on their behalf.

requirements.statement: The post-normalization key must match KEY_RE /^[A-Z][A-Z0-9]*$/; failure prints to stderr and exits 2.
requirements.why: Only KEY_RE-clean keys produce headings the parser can bind.

requirements.statement: spec domain new <KEY> creates spec-engine/<KEY>/SPEC.json as a complete, valid domain envelope — key, owner null, specVersion 1, and the current date in the author's local timezone — so spec index ingests the new domain immediately with zero diagnostics.
requirements.why: A scaffold must be born valid: a file the indexer cannot parse yields a domain with zero requirements and no error, and a UTC-computed date stamps evening authors with tomorrow.

requirements.statement: spec domain new <KEY> refuses to overwrite an existing spec-engine/<KEY>/SPEC.json — it prints an error to stderr and exits 2.
requirements.why: Never clobber an authored spec.

requirements.statement: The resolved scaffold destination must stay inside the platform directory; violation exits 2.
requirements.why: Path-containment defense in depth even though KEY_RE already blocks traversal characters.

requirements.statement: A domain is a child directory of spec-engine/ containing a SPEC.json, enumerated from the filesystem — never from the derived index or a cache file.
requirements.why: Canonical truth is the git spec location; a second derived store would violate the derived-DB constraint.

requirements.statement: spec domain list prints the keys sorted lexicographically, one per line, exit 0; zero domains produce empty output and still exit 0.
requirements.why: Authors need to see what exists; prefix resolution builds on this listing.

requirements.statement: spec domain list on a missing or non-directory spec-engine/ prints the friendly not-a-platform message and exits 2.
requirements.why: Same command-boundary pattern as map/index/check.

requirements.statement: spec req matches its domain prefix case-insensitively against the filesystem domain listing, via the same normalization as domain new.
requirements.why: Removes the exact-uppercase-key friction from next-id lookup.

requirements.statement: An exact domain match wins over longer prefix matches; otherwise a unique prefix resolves.
requirements.why: auth must resolve to AUTH even when AUTHX exists.

requirements.statement: An ambiguous prefix prints the candidate list to stderr and exits 2.
requirements.why: Guessing between BILLING and BOOKING would author into the wrong domain.

requirements.statement: A no-match prefix prints the available domains to stderr and exits 2; a nonexistent domain no longer mints a <KEY>-001 id.
requirements.why: Resolution runs against existing domains only — spec domain new is the way to create one.

requirements.statement: spec req <domain-prefix>, run non-interactively with piped stdin, prints the next unused id — the domain's SPEC.json max sequence + 1, padded to three digits, as <KEY>-<NNN> on stdout — and exits 0.
requirements.why: IDs are never reused; the id query must agree with the domain envelope's requirements, not a regex approximation.

requirements.statement: spec req on a non-platform directory prints the friendly not-a-platform message and exits 2.
requirements.why: Same boundary contract as AUTHC-009.

requirements.statement: Exit codes across the authoring surfaces are 0 and 2 only; exit 1 stays reserved for spec check --ci semantic failures.
requirements.why: CI must be able to distinguish gate failures from authoring/usage errors.

requirements.statement: spec new and spec id are removed entirely — no cli.ts entries, absent from --help, no aliases, and invoking them has no scaffold or next-id side effect.
requirements.why: Nothing is published, so back-compat shims carry no value and would re-open the dash trap.

requirements.statement: When stdin is a TTY, spec req enters the interactive per-field prompt flow with prompts rendered to stderr; the allocated id and Active status are displayed but not editable.
requirements.why: stdout stays machine-parseable while humans author interactively.

requirements.statement: When stdin is not a TTY, spec req prints the next unused id and exits 0 — no prompts, no writes, no readline.
requirements.why: The composable id query for scripts and agents is preserved byte-for-byte.

requirements.statement: An empty Requirement answer aborts with a stderr notice and exit 0, writing zero bytes.
requirements.why: Git editor-abort tradition — an abandoned edit is not an error.

requirements.statement: spec req <KEY> --text appends a new Active requirement to the domain's SPEC.json — status active, the given statement, why null, empty relates/livesIn/issues, changedAtVersion 1 — and bumps the envelope's updated to the local date, such that the appended entry re-indexes with zero diagnostics.
requirements.why: Authoring must produce exactly what the indexer ingests, or authored requirements silently vanish.

requirements.statement: An @-ref is @<relative/path> preceded by start-of-string or whitespace, containing at least one slash, resolved against the platform root; emails, @spec tags, and version pins never match.
requirements.why: Requirement text points at code without false positives on everyday @ usage.

requirements.statement: At authoring time each field answer is scanned for @-refs; an unresolvable ref warns on stderr and never blocks the save.
requirements.why: A requirement may legitimately point at code that does not exist yet.

requirements.statement: At index time, each @-ref in requirement field lines that does not resolve to a file under the platform root emits one warning-severity BROKEN_FILE_REF diagnostic with platform-relative source, real line, and enclosing requirement id; traversal outside the root is broken regardless of existence.
requirements.why: Containment before existence — defense in depth; stale refs surface without failing the build.
